sentences.text: Yes.
That was so much better than in Auschwitz, of course, because at least we had some civilized way toilet.
I mean, that was wooden toilets with the little cubicles, so we wasn't seeing each other.

And we had some days when we was-- when the weather turned better, I remember we was outside of the building, and I just kept on looking at how people could escape, because I see on one side of the building there was a leak or some water, which [INAUDIBLE] right to the building.
But the windows were so small that nobody could get out on that windows, because I see the other side of the-- that water quite a distance away.
It's houses, and on-- on that side where I was standing, where the dry land was, I seen-- I only could see above the gates.
And I seen mountains and I seen the train go in.
And I thought how about somebody managed to-- I mean, we had-- we owned nothing.
Somebody could write a big note-- you know, help, or something like that.
I don't know who was on those trains.
Would anybody recognize?
We were standing on the grounds outside.
We was waiting for-- for another set of this stripe uniform [INAUDIBLE].
It wasn't very good material, because it was fraying out so much.
This was in Mauthausen that you're talking--
This was Mauthausen, yes, and was the very start being good.
And when we wasn't sleeping and wasn't go to work, we could be in the courtyard, but not-- not standing in five by five, like Auschwitz.
We could just mingle about.
And I was just thinking about it.
I mean, there's people around.
And they don't know we are here.
How could they?
And I was saying to a girl who was a bit older than me, I said, this is the people live around this area.
I mean, they don't know we are here.
How could they let them know?

And she said, there was somebody-- because she understood German-- and somebody heard when these German soldiers were talking to one another at work-- you know, where we was working-- they were scared a few people who tried to get out climbing the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the fence at night.
And somebody somehow got out on that window, and they was swimming for a-- [INAUDIBLE].
And they got him.
And [INAUDIBLE].
You know, they was boasting to one another.
You know, I'm going to have another medal.

It's really frightening, because everybody had most likely-- not just me-- you know, I'd wonder how could we get out, because we were surrounded with people who wasn't that far, and they might not see we are there.
I didn't attempt, no.
But I would think about it too.
What I was thinking about it was some clothes-- you know, take off and write in big letters, you know, we are here.
And also in camp Mathausen, again, was a washroom with no windows in it.
This was all broken.
There was only cold water in it.
It was awfully difficult to-- to clean ourselves, really.
It was absolutely terrible.
Only at work, but I mean, you couldn't undress.
You could wash your face, hair.
But not any other area, because these men were coming and going who worked there.
And-- and the soldiers-- you couldn't tell how long they would be away.
Just quick, quick, you know, go under the tap, rinse our hair and back, because we had only a little hair then, you know.
It wasn't growing much yet.
Don't know.
It was the factory.
But they definitely was there with wool, as well, and dying wool, because some people who went to work to that area, they told me what they was doing.
And their hands used to be being black, because all-- even if they used any colored dye, their-- their hands would been colored dark.
So you know-- in the same big, big work area, you know they was dealing with cotton wool and wool.
I don't know what else they was doing.
I only know what I [INAUDIBLE] and the people told me.
And we was-- I personally, because when I was a child, I used to be in drawing.
I [INAUDIBLE] pencil.
But at the time when these Germans disappeared, and we're left to ourselves, I was taking out this brown paper from my-- my bag, which I was laying on.
And I smooth it out, and I was drawing how we was looked, you know, with this stripe uniform.
I was drawing ourselves, you know, how we was look like.
Did you make any relationships with people in Mauthausen?
No.
No, we were--
It was very difficult.
Everybody was so tired and frightened.
And there's always soldiers about.
You know, you couldn't really.
I mean, maybe they didn't understand Hungarian, but nevertheless, they-- they seen us talking, and we wasn't not supposed to.
Yeah, and then that was the time when-- when the Germans went.
And-- and then the American soldiers came in.
And they-- they went and got the Red Cross ladies and their bus-- a small, small bus, because there wasn't very many of us left by then.
Approximately how many?
Do you know?
I don't know.
The small bus was full, but when we went-- I mean, my number was-- I can't remember now-- 200 or 300, around like that.
And they start with number one.
So I mean, we most likely have been several hundreds.
And it was only that that bus full of people who survived-- you know, that one bus.
And we was taking him to this place, where it was called Hitler Jugend's training place or what.
But it was a very pleasant place.
And the surrounding was beautiful.
It was Austria, because camp Mauthausen is Austria.
So it was quite close to Mauthausen, was it?
I think it is in Austria.
I think.
I'm not sure.
I really don't know.
I think it is in Austria.
And it's like him olden days.
The prisoners had to put their finger over-- some or both-- I don't remember.
They asked questions like name and [INAUDIBLE] where they come from and age.
And they had-- instead of signing, they had to put in the ink pad our fingers and put it on that document.
And that's why I got it from the Red Cross-- the place and date, you know, where I was.
Because before that, I-- I wouldn't know what day it was.
And I was-- wasn't aware.
We start having food.
But they give us really-- we had a bath and clean clothes and shoes and lovely food and everything.
But nobody could eat very much, because, you know, the stomach is gone.
And I remember giving me a glass of milk, and I couldn't finish it because I felt I would be sick if I would finish it.
And they arranged it.
Of course, there were several people like me, who wanted to go back to Hungary, thought maybe their parents is alive.
And you know, these women around me, they knew my mother wasn't alive.
They didn't told me.
And-- otherwise, I wouldn't have gone back.
I wouldn't wanted to go back, because I heard from my father before the war we have relatives in America-- his cousins, who settled in America turn of the century.
So if I would knew I don't find nobody, I wouldn't gone back.
So anyway are going back, and I was-- To Budapest.
Budapest.
No, because they disappeared.
I mean, they wasn't nowhere.
And these ladies from the Red Cross, they's taken over that area for re-established people who are coming out of camps.
Maybe they came-- they was looking after people in the same place before we came, because was the last one who find.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when I went back to Hungary, to Budapest, it was straight, really, into a hospital, because my skin was covered in spots and my tummy grown so big.
I was really-- I don't know how I was looking.
I was looking most likely awful.
Yes, they-- they said this is the general-- not everybody had it such a bad way, but that's the average when people restart eating.
Then the system can't cope with it.
And they give me some cream and bandage on me all over, you know.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they didn't give me anything for my tummy.
Just give me some light food-- you know, sort of boiled egg and just light food, you know-- semolina pudding, and you know, just light food.
Wouldn't think of giving me any heavy food, you know.
And I was quite a while in the hospital.
I don't know how long.
But I was quite a while.
But there was, again, some people who was coming and organizing what happened with people after leave the hospital.
So I was put into a home where it was all young people who had no parents.
It's not necessarily because of the war.
It can be because the mother left them for some reason.
How did you feel at this time, being on your own like that?
I tell you that.
I said I never pray again.
I never put my feet into a synagogue.
And I never tell anybody I'm Jewish.
And I have no-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't believe in a God who allowed to that happen.
And I'm afraid.
It's still with me.
Yeah, I can't help it, but that's the way I am.
It is-- it's terrible.
When the people talking about their religious-- people taking about their Messiah-- Messiah, but if he hadn't came when was needed, don't bother to come now.
It's too late.
So anyway, in this home, we had clean everything and food.
And we all were sent to learn something.
So I was learned in a factory for two years as a learner for-- it is was a menswear factory, but what was it?
It's like conveyor belt the clothes was going, so I was just on one or another section.
So I don't know what was the date when I became-- not a learner, because I kept doing the same thing.
And I did marry young-- to a young man who worked there.
But he joined the army soon after.
So we didn't see each other because the communist [INAUDIBLE], you know.
And the army training was like that.
You know, they had to be secret places and not seeing each other.
So we didn't see each other very much.
And then in the top, he got a title, so he could have army home.
But I didn't want to leave Budapest.
I was working in a-- it's a-- it's a parent company of this place where I was a learning.
But it was a big nearer to the flat where I lived.
And I was making women's coats.
And I was very glad.
And the whole shift was three shifts.
One shift was all for under 25s.
And I was quite happy to be there.
And after the war.
Nobody asked anybody what religion, and that suited me really well.
And anyway, 1956, when the uprising was Hungary, I just came out with several other people without he knew about it.
He was in the army.
But--

Yes, I feared soon when I was in Hungary, and I met up with people who knew what happened to my mother, if I had known then my mother isn't going to be fine when I go back, I would never have gone back to Budapest or to Hungary.
I would look for relatives who is not in Hungary.
And I didn't want to go back.
I Got no good memories of Hungary, because the Hungarians, unfortunately, are very anti-Jewish feeling, you know.
And I think they are still like these days, because I heard they will go to holiday, and-- who understand the Hungarian language-- they do find remarks still.
No, because they wasn't allowed-- neither Christian nor Jewish religion-- to be taught.
So there's no young people really learn neither.
All these buildings was open only like museums.
You could go in.
If you want to pray, pray.
If not, not.
But it was no service.
And the synagogue where in this outskirts of Budapest where my parents lived-- you know, my family lived-- I went back there on the very beginning.
And I went to that synagogue.
And I saw about-- they had 10 people at the most who-- who was there, and everybody asked one another, did you heard of my this name and that name, and nobody-- nobody.
And it was very painful, because I remember occasionally on high holy days, my grandmother was taking me to-- my mother didn't-- my grandmother taking me to the synagogue.
And it was jam packed.
You know-- women upstairs.
Men downstairs.
I mean, there was nobody.
And everybody saw-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] destroyed mentally, really, because as we recovered slowly, you know, but mentally it's very hard to come to terms with what happened.
It's impossible.
Yeah.
Yes, somebody was lives there.
And I heard later on from people what happened when we left-- it-- all valuables was taken by the government or whoever.
I don't know.

But then, in places we left open, what nobody find it with the key, and then the general public just can go and have their selves, you know, like saucepans and plates and, you know, whatever didn't find it valuable, the government, of the time.
But then-- but then a remark I heard-- there is no home in Hungary, which haven't got some property from a Jewish home.
It sort of make it like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know-- it's a-- it's a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] something to be proud of or something.
I don't know.
It's terrible.
It's really terrible to know that to have in their homes things from people who've been killed.
This is terrible.
It's later on I heard.
So as later-- later on, more and more I heard of, you know, which I didn't know then.
You know, I had no idea what happened to my father's business, because it was expensive machinery, which he worked since he was 16, and my mother, as well, because they was very young when they met.
My mother worked as a dressmaker, and he was worked around the clock in manufacturing somewhere.
So they all worked extremely hard to have what they . had been just taken away.
And the only thing I want to say.
Jews wasn't fighting.
Jews wasn't taking guns and fighting.
So they can't be really regarded as war-- anything to do with the war.
Or just didn't want to say a certain part of a country where they were.
They want to declare it is theirs.
So it just wasn't in the army either.
So I mean, how can be the Jews of these European countries-- that I know Hungary.
They was not done anything which made them-- the government of that time-- make them unsuitable to be in the country, because they wasn't doing anything.
He was just earning their keep.
And that's all they was doing.
He wasn't involved into anything.
So can't compare it to civil wars of other countries where people are fighting for some reason with guns.
In 1956, yes.
And that was a frightening experience, because I couldn't walk on the streets.
I was living on a main-- it was a flat on a main road, which is right in the center of Budapest.
And there was a red store, which does the printing for one of these Hungarian newspapers.
And people was climbing up and pulling down the ropes and the trumps, which goes on the street-- they were smashed windows and barricades was on the-- on the railway lines.
You know, you couldn't go A to B.
And everything was-- all windows-- this is main road, I'm talking about.
The windows were smashed.
You couldn't get any food.
It was really frightening.
A radio station was on, constantly was saying-- constantly with music, so you couldn't tell what's going on.
Nobody knew what's happening.
So I don't know even today why it was starting, who done it, and what was happening.
So it was [INAUDIBLE] for some really lengthy time.
I don't know how long-- some weeks.
It was going on that when friends decided to go to the border town, which one near to Austria, because heard from other paper down there.
So we had some kind of transfer to get to the Austrian border, last town in Hungary.
And everybody throwed away their identity card and walked through on the no man's land, which I'm told was mines on it.
Luckily, we didn't step on any of them.
And on the other side in Austria was waiting the Red Cross-- again [LAUGHS] the Red Cross.
And they had [SOBBING] [INAUDIBLE] never go back to Hungary ever, because what they'd done to let us-- they-- they offer us to the Germans, the Hungarian government then-- government.
They really offer to help to take us out.
Actually, I do remember seeing the Germans marching into Hungary because we lived in the main road with this Nazi salute.

And the other thing that side-- you know, the motor bicycles with the side cars-- if I see any like that, it makes me shiver, because now and then I see a motor bicycle with this sidecar. it's really making me shiver.
It reminds me this-- the start when the Germans arrive to Hungary and all that was arranged to get us out.
You see?
I would never go back to Hungary ever.
And I really don't understand those Jews who've gone back to Germany.
I really don't know understand them.

It wasn't like that choice, you see, because I've heard I could learn the language in English easier than any other language, because when we came out the Red Cross took us to a place where was everybody.
They asked name and address, and you know, what job they can do, so they sort them out who is going where.
Well, I had not any other job than working in the dressmaking factory.
And I find that to learn any other language than English, it would be difficult.
And I said I would like to come to England or any English-speaking country.
And that was-- England is where I came.
I did mention I have relatives in America, but I couldn't tell the name and address, so they couldn't contact them.
And I know.
I remember.
It says it was Ohio, but I mean.
It's a big place and how they can find them?
And of course, everybody who had some funny name was most changed it, you know, because Hochhauser is not an easy name to say, you know.
So I came to England, and I divorced from that first marriage and married to my second husband in 1963.
I got two children, and one is not married.
[SOBBING] The other on is in Israel. who-- who went-- I wanted to go to Israel.
Wouldn't have me because I haven't got the money to keep myself, because I-- because I'd taken on the British National, really.
And they only have space for those who is in danger.
So after my [INAUDIBLE] the Israeli government wouldn't have me unless I keep myself.
So everybody who is-- who is a Jewish person can go to live in Israel, but they have to keep themselves.
So if you hear-- What do you mean by that?
Do you mean they have to-- to earn a living to themselves, or--
[INAUDIBLE].
Either have a chance to earn their living, or they have any income.
But they can't support-- they can't support anybody who is not coming from a country where they are in danger, because the country is very beginning, and they can't afford to support people.
So it's really hurtful, but because under 32 can go to work in a kibbutz, that's different.
So my daughter as a volunteer, actually.
She was only going six weeks for her between study.
And she says, well, I'll take a year out, because I find other people who take a year out.
And she met her husband, and she stay.
She's there.
But it's really hurtful for me not to be able to go to live in Israel is really-- is very hurtful.
I-- I find that really this is a very bad for me, because I should really live there.
What can I do?
This is [INAUDIBLE].
It's nothing to do with the-- with the extra number of Russians came out.
That was well before.
When my ex-husband-- 1977-- decided we leave-- he'd leave and we'd separate, that was the time when I started.
They said if I'm over 32, they are not able to help me.
